summary: I was born in Nancy, France in 1936 and spent my early childhood there. During World War II, my family lived through the German occupation in Nancy. It was in this town that General Patton's Third United States Army came through and American GIs were stationed, some of whom we hosted in our home. Nancy holds many memories for me, from my father's gardening to the difficult war years and the arrival of the Allies.

World War II shaped much of my childhood, especially living in occupied France. My father joined the French Army and was taken as a German prisoner of war, which was a very hard time for my family. When I was eight, the Allies liberated France, and that moment brought hope and change to our lives. The presence of American soldiers during the war introduced me to a new culture and kindness that I would later embrace.

My father served in the French Army during World War II, which meant he had to leave us during those difficult years. Unfortunately, he was captured and became a German prisoner of war, which was very challenging for our family. His service and sacrifice were part of the larger struggle in France during the war. I always respected his role and the hardships he endured for our country.

The Allies played a crucial role in liberating France when I was a child. I vividly remember the day when American soldiers arrived in our town as part of the Allied forces. Their presence brought relief and kindness, and my family even hosted some of the American GIs in our home. The Allies' victory marked a turning point in my life and in the history of France.

General George S. Patton was a name I heard often during the liberation of my town in France. His Third United States Army passed through Nancy when I was eight years old, bringing American soldiers who were welcomed by my family. I remember the kindness of the troops and the gifts they brought, like Hershey bars and peanut butter. Patton's leadership was part of the Allied effort that changed our lives forever.

The Third United States Army, led by General Patton, came through my hometown of Nancy during World War II. I was just a child then, but I remember the excitement and hope their arrival brought. My family hosted some of the soldiers, and they shared food and kindness with us. That experience was my first real encounter with Americans and left a lasting impression on me.

After the war, I worked as a secretary for the United States Army starting in 1953, using my skills in French, German, and English. This job connected me to the American military community and eventually led to my marriage to an American GI. The US Army was a bridge between my French past and my new life in America. It was through this connection that I moved to Minnesota and embraced a new culture.

Virginia, Minnesota became my new home in 1958 after I married an American GI and moved from France. It was a big change for me, leaving behind my family and the life I knew. The people there were welcoming, though their food and customs were very different from what I was used to. Living in Virginia marked the beginning of my American journey and adapting to a new culture.

After some years in Virginia, Minnesota, I later lived in Columbia Heights, Minnesota. This was another chapter in my life in America where I continued to build friendships and raise my family. Columbia Heights was part of my experience adapting to American life while keeping my French heritage alive through cooking and traditions.

Eventually, after remarrying, I moved to North Carolina, which was a new place for me in the United States. This move happened later in my life, and it represented another transition and fresh start. North Carolina became part of my American story, where I continued to embrace both my French roots and my life in the US.

When I first arrived in Minnesota, many of the people I met were Finnish Americans. Their foods, like pickled herring, hot-dish, and oatmeal, were very different from what I was used to in France. It was a cultural adjustment for me, but I learned to appreciate the diversity around me. Meeting Finnish Americans helped me understand the rich immigrant tapestry of Minnesota.

My children grew up wanting Betty Crocker Angel Food cakes for their birthdays, which was a fun American tradition. Over time, though, they began to prefer the French desserts I made, like meringue tortes. Betty Crocker represented a part of American culture that I embraced while still sharing my French culinary heritage with my family. It was a sweet blend of both worlds.
